title: Nomewaimea urerana Austin & Rubinoff 2026
author: Austin, Kyhl A.
license_note: Zenodo record metadata license field: cc-zero
description: Nomewaimea urerana  (Swezey, 1915), comb. nov.   Fig. 4C, D, I   Epagoge urerana  Swezey, 1915: 93.   Spheterista urerana  (Swezey, 1915): Zimmerman, 1978: 454; figs. 232, 243, 253.   Diagnosis.   Nomewaimea urerana  is most similar to  Nomewaimea infaustana  (Walsingham). At present, we can find no consistent characters to separate them from each other (but see Comments below). The terminal plate of  N .  urerana  may be slightly more slender and the valvae more apically extended than in  N .  infaustana , but these characters may be somewhat variable.   Type material.   Epagoge urerana  Swezey, 1915.  Lectotype  [designated by Zimmerman, 1978], ♂.  Oʻahu:  Tantalus, O. H. S.; 5 iv [19]14 / ex. stem of  Urera  / genitalia on slide Z-I-26-67 (BPBM) [examined].  Paralectotypes  [designated by Zimmerman, 1978] (3♂♂).  Oʻahu:  1♂, same as lectotype (BPBM) [not examined]. 2♂♂, same as lectotype (USNM) [examined].   Additional material examined  (44♂♂, 47♀♀).  Oʻahu:  11♂♂, 5♀♀, Round Top For[est] Res[erve], Mānoa Cliff Restoration Area; 21.3379, -157.8108; 555 m; 26–27 xi 2021; K. A. Austin, K. Faccenda; [UV] bucket trap / DNA extractions KA0420, KA0422 / KAA diss. #0683 (♂), #0685 (♀) (UHIM). 12♂♂, 12♀♀, same as previous except 21.3376, -157.8108; 565 m / DNA extractions KA0419, KA0421 / KAA diss. #0682(♂), #0684(♀) (UHIM). 1♂, same as previous except 21.3381, -157.8110; 560 m; UVLED light sheet (UHIM). 1♂, Round Top For[est] Res[erve], Mānoa Cliff Restoration Area; 21.3381, -157.8110; 560 m; larva coll. 26 xi 2021, adult ecl. 8 xii 2021; K. A. Austin, K. Faccenda / HOST:  Pipturus albidus  (Urticaceae), feeding in young terminal shoots (UHIM). 2♂♂, Honolulu Watershed F[orest] R[eserve]; Mānoa Cliff Restoration Area; 21.3375, -157.8109; 570 m; 15 iii 2023; K. Szczygieł; [UV] light trap (UHIM). 1♀, Pahole N[atural] A[rea] R[eserve], n[ea]r summit ridge; 21.5417, -158.1935; 600 m; 19–20 ii 2023; K. A. Austin; UV bucket trap (UHIM). 1♂, 1♀, Kaʻala N[atural] A[rea] R[eserve], bog boardwalk; 21.5070, -158.1440; 1210 m; 21–22 i 2022; K. A. Austin, K. Faccenda, T. Chapin; [UV] bucket trap (UHIM). 1♀, Waiʻanae Kai For[est] Res[erve], M[oun]t Kaʻala, near south end of bog boardwalk; 1200 m; 21.5033, -158.1478; 10–11 iv 2021; K. A. Austin, M. San Jose, K. Faccenda; UV bucket trap / DNA extraction KA0157 / KAA diss. #0455 (UHIM). 1♂, 5♀♀, Waiʻanae Kai For[est] Res[erve], Kaala Trail; 21.5027, -158.1495; 1140 m; 4–5 ix 2021; K. A. Austin, K. Faccenda; [UV] bucket trap / DNA extractions KA0315, KA0316, KA0317 / KAA diss. #0607(♂), #0608(♀), #0609(♀) (UHIM). 1♀, same as previous except 21.5027, -158.1505; 1050 m (UHIM). 1♀, Mākua Keaʻau F[orest] R[eserve], ʻŌhikilolo, through gate; 21.5134, -158.1932; 890 m; 30 iv–1 v 2022; K. A. Austin, K. Faccenda; [UV] bucket trap (UHIM). 2♂♂, Honouliuli F[orest] R[eserve], Puʻu Hāpapa, north of snail jail; 21.4670, -158.1029; 810 m; 14–15 iii 2022; K. A. Austin; [UV] bucket trap / DNA extraction KA0553 / KAA diss. #0785 (UHIM). 2♂♂, 1♀, Honouliuli F[orest] R[eserve], Puʻu Hāpapa, behind weatherport; 21.4667, -158.1028; 810 m; 14–15 iii 2022; K. A. Austin; UVLED light sheet (UHIM). 2♀♀, Honouliuli F[orest] R[eserve], ʻĒkahanui Gulch; 21.4389, -158.0939; 675 m; 24–25 v 2023; K. A. Austin  et al. ; UV bucket trap / DNA extraction KA0827 / KAA diss. #1057 (UHIM). 1♂, 1♀, same as previous except 21.4390, -158.0935; 655 m (UHIM). 1♀, Nānākuli Forest Reserve, Palikea Ridge; 21.4129, -158. 0999; 846 m; M. San Jose, J. B. Reil, K. A. Austin; 11–12 ii 2021; UV bucket trap / DNA extraction KA0119 / KAA diss. #0431 (UHIM). 3♂♂, 5♀♀, Makaleha, M[oun]t Kaʻala; 8 i 1922; Swezey / ex.  Pipturus  (BPBM). 2♂♂, 2♀♀, same as previous (HDOA). 1♂, Halona Val[ley], Waianae M[oun]t[ain]s; 12 iii [19]33; O. H. Swezey (HDOA).   Comments.  Zimmerman (1978) wrote, “This species was described from four specimens reared from larvae. There were two male examples on the cork mount marked ‘Type’, and evidently Dr. Swezey considered them to be male and female. As my illustrations demonstrate, however, they are both males. I have designated the specimen nearest the pin as the lectotype male and have removed the other specimen from the mount and designated it as a paralectotype. The specimens are in Bishop Museum. I have not studied a female.” We have been unable to locate this paralectotype in BPBM.  Though we were unable to observe consistent morphological differences between  N .  urereana  and  N .  infaustana , we choose to maintain them as distinct species (see explanation under the Comments section of  N .  infaustana  above).   Biology.  Apart from the type series from “  Urera sandwicensis  ” (=  Touchardia oahuensis , ōpuhe), all other reared specimens have been found feeding on the shoots, stems, and leaves of māmaki ( Pipturus albidus ; Fig. 4I).  Touchardia oahuensis  is now a somewhat rare species on Oʻahu, whereas māmaki is still relatively common. It is possible that  N .  urerana  can utilize other native Urticaceae, such as  Boehmeria grandis  and  Touchardia latifolia , but we have not encountered these plants frequently enough to confirm this. See Swezey (1915) for a brief description of the larva, pupa, and feeding habits.   Distribution.   Nomewaimea urerana  has been collected throughout much of the Koʻolau and Waiʻanae Ranges between elevations of ca. 500–1200 m. It is most common in wet forest and gulches, but can be occasionally found in more shaded, mesic areas wherever native Urticaceae grow.   Conservation status.  Austin & Rubinoff (2023a) presumed  N .  urerana  extinct and offered two competing hypotheses to explain its disappearance: “This species may be a synonym of ‘  S .’  infaustana  (Walsingham), described from Kauaʻi. Alternatively, the type series could be an unusual color form of a widespread extant species on Oʻahu closely related to ‘  S .’  infaustana  but provisionally considered undescribed by KAA. It is still common on parts of Tantalus where  Pipturus  and  Urera  grow.” Austin & Rubinoff (2024b) reported  N .  urerana  as rediscovered, but did not elaborate. The second hypothesis of Austin & Rubinoff (2023a) is partially correct: the type series of  N .  urerana , specifically the lectotype, is indeed unusual in its pale banding. However, we recognize now that there is just a single species of  Nomewaimea  on Oʻahu,  N .  urerana .   Nomewaimea urerana  is still widespread and relatively common.Although it can likely be considered secure for now, invasive ramie moths ( Arcte coerula  (Guenée); Noctuidae) pose a potentially significant threat to  Nomewaimea urerana  and other native insects that depend on native Urticaceae.
